But the blue bar is only 20 columns wide, so it can't cover both.
What if the goal is to move the blue bar so that it's centered between the two yellow blocks? The gap between the yellow blocks is cols 28-39 (12 columns). Wait, cols 24-43 overlaps with cols 16-27 (cols 24-27) and cols 40-51 (cols 40-43). So the blue bar would overlap with both yellow blocks.

Actually, let me reconsider. The blue bar at cols 28-47 overlaps with the right yellow block at cols 40-51 (cols 40-47). It doesn't overlap with the left yellow block at cols 16-27.
If I move the blue bar to cols 24-43, it would overlap with the left yellow block at cols 16-27 (cols 24-27) and the right yellow block at cols 40-51 (cols 40-43). So the blue bar would overlap with both yellow blocks.
But wait, the blue bar is at rows 16-19, and the yellow blocks are at rows 52-59. They're not at the same rows, so they don't overlap spatially. The blue bar is above the yellow blocks.

Actually, wait. Let me look at the animation again. The blue bar disappears in frame 0, and the magenta starts expanding. The magenta object is at cols 36-39, rows 1-7. When it expands, it grows to 592px. The yellow blocks are at cols 16-27 and 40-51, rows 52-59.
The magenta expansion seems to be a "beam" that starts from the target and spreads downward. The yellow blocks disappear when the beam reaches them.
What if the blue bar is supposed to "intercept" the beam? If the blue bar is positioned correctly, it might block the beam from reaching the yellow blocks. But in the animation, the blue bar disappears, so it doesn't block the beam.

- Blue bar in middle (moves horizontally and vertically)
- Two yellow blocks at bottom
